title: Hightower Petroleum Corporation Report to Shareholders
description: A corporate report to the shareholders of the Hightower Petroleum Corporation.
transcription:
Page 1| 

{illegible, top-most line on the upper-left corner} 

Glen Rose {illegible, right-adjacent to previous} 

Glen Rose, Texas 

 

Glen Rose 

3 30PN 

JUN28 

1950 

Texas 

 

Mr. Allen H Jones 

Senoia, Ga. 

 

Page 2| 

1810 CALIFORNIA STREET 

DENVER 2, COLORADO 

 

HIGHTOWER PETROLEUM CORPORATION 

 

THE 2 FOR 1 PLAN 

 

Facing Page of report to stockholders of Hightower Petroleum Corporation: 

 

Foreword: 

 

There is being sent you as stockholders of Hightower Petroleum Corporation a report which is attached and to be considered herewith: 

 

The original offering of Hightower Petroleum Corporation, sales under which was discontinued after Jan. 1950, was described in Hightower Petroleum Corporations prospectus of July 15, 1949 as follows. 

 

The offering: 

 

The Corporation is now offering 3,000,000 shares at par value of 10¢ each to gross the treasury $300,000. The Corporation will net about $210,000 after paying an estimated total distribution cost of $90,000 which will amount to approximately 30 per cent of the 3,000,000 shares to be sold at 10¢ each, or about three cents per share. 

 

J. Paul Hightower originally made avaliable from his personal holdings 1,000,000 shares to be distributed as a bonus to the first 4,000 subscribers, and these bonus shares were limited to 250 shares to each subscriber. The minimum purchase was held to 250 shares and the maximum to 750 shares, exclusive of the bonus shares. 

 

As of April 30, 1949, when gross sales had reached $84,165, the distribution cost was approximately 46½ percent or 4.65¢ per share. Obviously, the limit on subscriptions was placing a hardship on the company and the limit was lifted. It was believed the 2,300 shareholders would average purchasing 1,000 shares each at 10¢, par value, amounting to $100, and thus bring the cost to well within the original 30 percent estimate. The company now has 2,450 shareholders. 

 

To make the offering still more attractive, J. Paul Hightower recently donated from his personal holdings an additional 500,000 shares. These bonus shares will be distributed on a one-for-one basis- one bonus share to each additional share purchased at 10¢ each, par value. 

 

The fund of $210,000 will be availed of, so far as can now be determined, to drill wells; to acquire tracts near proven oil lands, and other tracts of potential oil and gas possibilities; to maintain an office; to pay field scouts, geologists, production engineers, legal, accounting and clerical work, and other expenses incident to the operation of the Corporation; and to reimburse J. Paul Hightower, the Corporation's president, the sum of approximately $10,000 for monies advanced by him in the organization of the Corporation; in negotiating and acquiring properties, and for expenditures incident to the Corporation's affairs until it has funds from the sale of its securities. 

 

Subsequent to the above, and up to March 31, 1949, Mr. Hightower incurred miscellaneous expenses, such as car allowance, traveling expense, and other items amounting to $5,822.27, for which the corporation is to reimburse him. 

 

Because these securities are believed to be exempt from registration, they have not been registered with the Securities and Exchange Commissio; but such exemption, if available, does not indicate that the securities have been either approved or disapproved by the Commission or that the Commission has considered the accuracy or completeness of the statements of this communication. 

 

Page 3| 

The offering is being made direct by the corporation and it will bear as part of the distribution cost, the expense of printing, mailing, postage, stationary, clerical, Office rent, and other expenses incident to the offering. The Corporation has employed James T. Chiles to supervise and direct the financing campaign, and he is to be paid 10 per cent of the gross proceeds derived from the sale of stock. In addition, he is to receive, from the personal holdings of J. Paul Hightower, one share of Hightower Petroleum Corporation stock for every five shares sold. 

 

The general purpose of the offering is to carry on the Corporation's two-for-one drilling program - two wells on proven or offset acreage for each wildcat drilled - and to meet business obligations and general corporation expenses. 

 

Since you are to receive stock in the Oil Prospectus, Inc, for transfer of certain Hightower Petroleum Corporation properties, it is deemed advisable to give you the following information concerning the offering of that company, I have been supplied with the following which is being quoted for your information. 

 

The offering: 

 

"There is being offered 100,000 shares of the Capital Stock of the Oil Prospectors, Inc., of the par value of $1.00 per share; and it is estimated that from the proceeds of the sale of such stock, throughout the entire sales campaign, not more than 30% of such funds so raised will be expended in solicitation costs; The net proceeds of the sale of such stock will be used forthe drilling of oil wells, the purchase of drilling rigs or equipment, purchase of casing and other kinds of drilling supplies, tanks, storage, and the payment of workmen, drillers, contractors and employees of the issuer. Also the purchase of il and gas leases, oil and gas wells, the royalties, interest in oil and gas wells, lands, rental houses and offices, to be used in connection with the drilling, development and sales and distribution of oil and gas and minerals generally; and for the purpose of acquiring from Hightower Petroleum Corporation, oil, gas, and mining leases and personal property used therewith in Jack and Somervell Counties, Texas and elsewhere and for the purpose of completing wells on said leases in Jack and Somervell Counties; and for the purpose of maintaining an oil company generally." 

 

Page 4| 

1810 CALIFORNIA STREET 

DENVER 2, COLORADO 

 

HIGHTOWER PETROLEUM CORPORATION 

 

THE 2 FOR 1 PLAN 

 

Friends: 

 

This is my overdue REPORT to you. While I realize that I may perhaps deserve some degree of censure, in a way, for not having reported sooner, I believe that by the time you have finished reading all that I am herewith reporting any censure that you may perhaps have had in the back of your mind for me for the delay will be forgotten in the enthusiasm you will feel over what is hereinbelow reported to you. 

 

IT IS IMPORTANT THAT YOU READ THIS REPORT CAREFULLY. Please do not skip hastily and casually thru it. There is quite a lot to tell and I sincerely hope that you will take the time necessary to fully assimilate the entire message and each individual part of it. 

 

We started out with some PLANS and high hopes. But, as Robert Burns, the Scotch peasant-poet so wisely wrote: "The best-laid plans o' mice and men gae aft agley". And while our plans have seemed to at least temporarily miscarry, dark clouds sometimes prove to have silver lining, so that DESPITE WHATEVER QUALMS YOU MAY HAVE BEEN FEELING WITH REGARD TO TH EMONEY YOU HAVE PUT INTO SHARES IN HIGHTOWER PETROLEUM CORPORATION AND DESPITE THAT YOUR SOARING HOPES MAY HAVE SOMEWHAT WANED, I, in all good faith, believe that we are close to emerging from all difficulties and discouragements and that we have now - MORE REASON THAN EVER BEFORE TO HOPE FOR QUICK AND LARGE SUCCESS, or, at least, that SUCCESS may now be NEARER. 

 

I have closed our Denver office for the present. I have returned to Texas, and am mailing you this report from Glen Rose while attending to some very important business here in connection with the so-called "Hightower block" just east of town. OUT OF WHAT I HAVE BEEN DOING AND AM DOING HERE I BELIEVE MAY SHORTLY COME THE COMPLETION OF A DISCOVERY OIL WELL ON THIS BLOCK AND CONSEQUENT LARGE MONETARY RETURNS FOR THE HIGHTOWER PETROLEUM CORPORATION SHAREHOLDERS WHO HAVE PAID CASH FOR THEIR SHARES. 

 

Hightower Petroleum Corporation has not offered its shares, either publicly, or, to the shareholders, since Aug. 1949 - and no offer is now being made of the treasury shares of the Corporation - nor have I offered nor am I offering for sale any of my personal shares in the Corporation. 

 

It is an important part of what I want to report herein that you should understand that I have never drawn any salary from the Corporation, and in addition I have paid most of my expenses out of my personal funds, acquired in other business enterprises before the organization of this company. I have made no money from the organization of Hightower Petroleum Corporation, but, to the contrary, have put more than $20,000.00 of my personal funds into the Corporation. I mention this because I want you to understand that I have been, and am, entirely in good faith and very conscientious in undertaking, as I have, to organize and build a successful oil compnay with this enterprise. I have been determined to make it a success, and I am still determined to do so. 

 

Page 5| 

- 2 - 

Toward you, the stockholders of this company, I feel a very deep sense of obligation. I want you to recognize that. And I want you to know full well that I am deeply sincere in my desire and my determination to fufill to you the obligation and stewardship that I feel. I HAVE NEVER CEASED FOR ONE DAY MY EFFORTS IN YOUR BEHALF AND I DO NOT INTEND TO CEASE SUCH EFFORTS SHORT OF ACHIEVING FOR YOU THAT MEASURE OF SUCCESS WHICH YOU EXPECT OF ME. 

 

Without attempting to go into a full, complete and detailed financial report AT THIS TIME, I want you to know, generally, that we have 2450 stockholders who have paid cash for stock in this company, which funds were used for printing, postage, and other expense and administration and for the acquisition and development of properties, getting wells drilled, having geological and geophyscial work done, etc. 

 

We did not complete our financing campaign, just as we did not complete our field-operations program, for reasons that need not be detailed here and now but which I assure you left no reflection upon my administration of the affairs of the company nor upon my personal good faith. 

 

You 2450 stockholders are entirely capable of furnishing the company all of the money it needs to make it a success, I am confident, and I am further confident that you would be prompt to furnish such further capital if called upon to do so, BUT I DID NOT WANT TO CALL ON YOU FOR FURTHER CAPITAL AND I DID NOT WANT TO GO FURTHER WITH THE COMPANY, AT ALL, UNTIL I COULD SUCCEED "ON MY OWN" IN OVERCOMING CERTAIN OBSTACLES WHICH HAD ARISEN.  

 

Since last writing you I have been diligently seeking to overcome these various obstacles and "clear the way" for the Corporation to go ahead with its original plans. Towards this achievement I have given unsparingly of time and effort and money, in your behalf, and with the sole objective of KEEPING FAITH WITH YOU. 

 

The outcome of the Wyoming well - drilled to a depth of 4543 feet - was a real disappointment to me. The total cost of that well was around $50,000.00 and we still owe balance, but the folks we dealt with are mighty fine and considerate and I am confident that this debt will be ultimately worked out satisfactorily to all concerned. I plan to settle that dept with my personal stock. 

 

The outcome of the Jack County well was in a way an even more severe disappointment to me and, to say the least, "certainly put a crimp in my plans", for I had counted most heavily on it making a producer and a GOOD producer. 

 

This well was drilled to a depth of around 4827 feet. It encountered oil and gas, and casing was set and the well produced an estimated about 300 barrels of high gravity oil, which I reported to you, with lots of gas, and I certainly thought it was a "cinch" for a splendid oil producer. Subsequently, you will recall, I reported to you that this well was a disappointment. "B-U-T" • • • NOW IT MAY BE A DIFFERENT STORY!!- as you'll see in a moment! 

 

Page 6| 

-3- 

 

The Hightower Petroleum Corporation actually paid out approximately $27,500.00 in the acquisition of this 130-acre property in Jack County, Texas, and the drilling of the well and equipment and work incident thereto. This means, of course, that you folks, the shareholders, spent that much money on the Jack County operation. 

 

Of the most supreme interest and importance, however, to all of us, was the Somervell County test well on our geophysical prospect there. (In oilmans language, a "geophysical prospect" is a block of leases which has been surveyed by some geophysical method and thereby disclosed to have underground features interpreted as favorable to the possible accumlation of oil and/or gas). 

 

We made a deal with Mr. Charles Kadane of Jacksboro, Texas, an independent oil operator, who has been quite successful, to drill this well, and he drilled it to a depth of 4,213 feet, at which point drilling with the rotary drilling rig was suspended and the hole temporarily plugged. The sum of money allocated to the drilling of this well was not sufficient to carry it deeper when it was evidenced that in this particular area drilling with rotary equipment was very expensive. 

 

It seems to me important that you should understand that the geophysical survey which was made with the gravity-meter disclosed a rather sharp sub surface anomaly or "area of difference", therefore the location for the well was made accordingly. The gravity-meter measures differences in the pull of gravity, and when such measurements are taken at various "stations" over a "grid-pattern" of an area and the results mapped so that a "picture" is disclosed that is contrued to be evidence of "structure", such "picture" is interpreted to be produced by massive beds of limestone. 

 

In the drilling of the well to around 4,200 feet, the Strawn series was not entirely penetrated and therefore the objective remained unreached, being the massive limestone beddings below the Strawn, probably Ellenburger lime. In my opinion, therefore, the drilling of the well to 4,200 feet was insufficient to reach the massive limes and therefore indeterminative so that TO HAVE A CHANCE TO SHOW WHETHER OIL IS PRESENT IN THE "STRUCTURE" INDICATED BY THE GRAVITY SURVEY. In my opinion it appears THE WELL NEEDS TO BE DRILLED AT LEAST ANOTHER THOUSAND FEET TO PENETRATE THE ELLENBURGER LIME FORMATION. 

 

What I wanted to do was to arrange for carrying the well on down to the Ellenburger lime with cable tools, if possible, because I consider percussian-type drilling more practical and economical and efficient in this particular series of formations in this area. 

 

So - - we came to the point where we had approximately 4800 feet of hole in the Jack County well - and both oil and gas in the well - and - a trifle over the 4,200 feet of hole made on the Somervell County block. And, including all lease and other incident expense, it cost around $75,000.00 to get these two holes drilled. 

 

I was "on my own". I did not want to ask you for money because I did not consider that the affairs of the Corporation were in such condition as to warrant me in offering corporation treasury shares for sale. I felt like it was up to me, personally, to "pull the company out of the hole" and to prove myself to you as worthy of the confidence you had placed in me.

title: Navy Photograph Hollbergs
description: A military photograph found in a miscellaneous Hollberg family album.

title: Advertisement for Senoia Area Historical Society Land Lottery Deeds on Display
description: A typed advertisement for SAHS describing the Land Lottery Deeds on Display.
transcription:
WE ARE ALL FAMILIAR WITH THE LOTTERY AND MOST OF US HAVE BOUGHT A TICKET HOPING FOR A CHANCE AT BIG MONEY, BUT DID YOU KNOW THAT ALMOST 3/4 OF THE LAND IN PRESENT DAY GA WAS DISTRIBUTED UNDER A LOTTERY SYSTEM?

IT WAS AN EARLY 19TH CENTURY SYSTEM OF LAND DISTRIBUTION IN GA, THE LARGEST HELD IN THE UNITED STATES.

WHITE MALES, ORPHANS, OR WIDOWS COULD APPLY.

8 TIMES BETWEEN THE YEARS OF 1805 AND 1833 A LOTTERY WAS HELD.

ROUGHLY 100,000 PEOPLE RECEIVED LAND IN GA AT BARGAIN PRICES.

TICKETS WERE PLACED IN A LARGE WOODEN DRUM CALLED A WHEEL.

THE WINNING TICKET CONTAINED A DISTRICT AND A LOT NUMBER, IT WAS TRADITIONALLY 202 1/2 ACRES OF LAND.

THE MUSEUM IS FORTUNATE TO HAVE 2 GA LAND LOTTERY DEEDS ON DISPLAY. THEY ARE DATED 1827 WHICH WOULD HAVE BEEN THE 5TH LAND LOTTERY. [Handwritten note] Which encompassed Coweta County.

WOW!

SO, IF YOU ARE LOOKING FOR A CHANCE TO HIT THE JACKPOT, VISIT THE SAHS MUSEUM. ITS A WINNER.

title: Written Notes on Hollberg Family History

title: "Senoia and My Family" by Frank Hollberg III

title: Newspaper Article "Frank Hollberg receives Legacy Award"
description: A newspaper article written by Lynne Wendt describing the SAHS presenting its inaugural Legacy Award to Frank Hollberg III.
transcription:
[Header]
SENIOR LIVING

[Body]
Frank Hollberg receives Legacy Award
By Lynne Wendt
SAHS President

The Senoia Area Historical Society (SAHS), presented its inaugural Legacy Award to Senoian Frank Hollberg III on Monday, Oct. 17. The award was given during a tribute dinner at Founders Restaurant and well attended by friends, relatives and employees of Mr. Hollberg.

Frank Hollberg is the owner and President of Hollberg's Fine Furniture. The Hollberg family has had a store in Senoia continuously since 1894. The Legacy Award recognized Mr Hollberg's lifetime of stewardship of the town and its citizens, and his patronage of the SAHS.

The dinner was advertised as a celebration to in honor of Senoia Founder's Day. Governor Joe Frank Harris declared Oct. 14, 1989 Sneoia Day in Georgia in recognition of the placement of the community on the National Register of Historic Places by the U.S. Secretary of the Interior in that year. Hollberg cane ti the dinner to celebrate Founder's Day and was surprised to see his relatives and employees there as well. Most everyone knew about the award except Mr. Hollberg, thanks to his lovely wife Bonnie, and we all enjoyed keeping it a secret.

When it was unveiled behind the podium, an enlarged photograph of young teens Frank Hollberg and Jim Baggarly in their Eagle Scout uniforms revealed the real reason for the evening's festivities. Todd Baggerly, the restaurant owner and descendant of Rev. Francis W. Baggarly, who founded the town of Senoia in 1860, had recently found the photograph in his family's collection and had enlarged it for display in the restaurant. The Boy Scouts of America ("BSA") were contacted about this award. The BSA sent a letter, read by Ms. Lynne Wendt, congratulating Mr. Hollberg on the award and reminding him that once an Eagle Scout, always an Eagle Scout. Hollberg was the first, or certainly one of the first, Eagle Scouts in the area.

One by one, long time friends of Hollberg's rose and went ot hte podium to reminisce about the old days in Senoia when they were young and Frank Hollberg was one of the boys. James Barnett, the third Senoia Eagle Scout, was first to speak, and revealed a secret that had been kept for decades. Sue McDaniel, who celebrated her 95th birthday in May, said in a very sincere voice what a privilege it had been for her to know Buddy, (Frank Hollberg's childhood nickname), these many years. Carl McKnight, Ellis Crook, Joyce Smith, Butch Reese, Jane Hutchinson, and Hal Sewell had their own stories to tell. Ellis Crook remembered that Frank had been called Buddy until he graduated from Georgia Tech, when he preferred his given name, Frank. Longtime Senoia resident Tray Baggerly brought the stories to a close, remembering his family's close relationship with the Hollberg family, and the mutual love he and Frank share for Georgia Tech sports. Gary Gruby rounded out the evening with the observation that all of the boys raised in Senoia have good manners, and then presented Mr. Hollberg with an original representational work of art that is now in his store's display window. IT was a lovely evening in Senoia, enjoying good food and fellowship with old friends, clapping Hollberg on the back, saying to him that he is truly a fine fellow, a point that everyone agreed was fact.

The stories were recorded by documentary filmmaker Alan Pogue, working with Gary Gruby Studios, and will be archived at the SAHS museum as part of its oral history project, "Stories of Senoia". Pogue has generously videotaped all of the storytellers for the SAHS.

[Photograph caption]
Photo by Alan Pogue
The Senoia Area Historical Society (SAHS), presented its inaugural Legacy Award to Senoian Frank Hollberg III, center, on Oct. 17.

title: Newspaper Article "Senoia Historical Society is staging annual fundraiser on September 7
description: An article from The Citizen describing the SAHS's annual fundraiser.
transcription:
[Title]
Senoia Historical Society is staging annual fundraiser on September 7

[Body]
It's time for Senoia Area Historical Society's annual Wine and Food pairing to be held Septemmber 7 at 7 p.m. at the society's house at 6 Couch Street in Senoia.

Pairing the right wine with the right food has a long and distinguished history. The society is proud to build on this tradition with its own wine and food pairing event. The society will present six extraordinary wines paired with six distinctive individual plates that will reveal the best aspect of the wine. Our Wine expert, Ed Izzo, will introduce each wine and answer any wine pairing questions you may have.r.

This is a fundraising event, and tickets are $50 per person for a total of 60 people. To reserve your place, contact cdivido@aol.com or call 770-727-9189

Senoia Area Historical Society is a 501(C)3 non-profit organization. All proceeds from the Wine & Food pairing will benefit the society and its "Raise the Roof" campaign. Any donations above ticket price will receive a receipt for a tax deductible donation.

[Photo Caption]
Visiting at last year's Wine Pairing sponsored by Senoia Area Historical Society are Rhonda Moye, Bonnie and Frank Hollberg.

title: Founder's Day Sale Letter/Advertisement
description: A letter sent out from Hollberg's Fine Furniture to advertise it's founder's day sale and announce the 90th birthday of C. Frank Hollberg, Jr.
transcription:
[Front of Card]

HOLLBERG'S FINE FURNITURE
33 Main Street
Senoia, Georgia 30276

Happy 90th Birthday

[Back of Card]
A Very Special affair...

[Interior]
[Letterhead]
Founder's Day Sale

[Captioned Under Pictures]
C.F. Hollberg III
C.F. Hollberg Jr.
C.F. Hollberg

[body]
July 5th--13th

Dear Preferred Customer and Friend:
On July 10th, C. Frank Hollberg, Jr. will celebrate his 90th birthday. In celebration of Dad's birthday we are offering special savings throughout the store. Many items will be marked down from 40% to 60% off manufacturer's list. All others will recieve an extra 10% Bonud off our regular low prices. As always, special orders will be 40% off.

During this special sale come in and see a piece of furniture that my Dad's father sold many, many years ago. Guess the month and year this piece was sold and receive a $500.00 Gift Certificate good for your next furniture purchase. In case of a tie we will draw the winner.

In addition, during our Founder's day Sale we will be giving away a $10.00 token for every purchase of 399.99 or more. Sorry, only one Token per customer. These tokens, which are copies of a 1910 token that my grandfather used in the old pharmacy, may be redeemed in trade anytime after August 1, 1991.

Sincerely yours,
HOLLBERG'S FINE FURNITURE
[Signature "Frank"]
C. Frank Hollberg, III

title: SAHS Letter to Frank Hollberg Jr
description: This postcard includes an image of "Princess Senoia" and a handwritten note to Frank Hollberg Jr.
archive_reference: LTR_SAHS Letter to Frank Hollberg Jr (w dvd of program about him)
transcription:
Dear Frank,

We are so glad to hear that you are doing well and getting back to Main Street. The house missed seeing you!

Here are some copies of the video from the Legacy Award Presentation to you. I hope that you enjoy the reply! 

Fondly,
Lynne Wendt

title: Letter to Frank Hollberg from Wallace Mitcham
description: A letter from Wallace J. Mitcham to Frank Hollberg reminiscing on his life in Senoia, GA.
archive_reference: LTR_Letter to Frank Hollberg from Wallace Mitcham
transcription:
Dear Mr. Frank: 

It gave me a great pleasure to read the write-up about the Hollbergs of Senoia. The article in the paper brought back fond memories of the Senoia, Ga.. that I remember as a small boy. So if you will excuse me I am going to reminisce for a minute. 

Tom Mitcham married Eunice Jones of Brooks, Ga. in brooks in 1911. They came to Senoia on their honeymoon. I don't have the date but it was shortly thereafter that Dad, (Tom) started to work for the Hollbergs. My Dad used to tell us children about the times he and your Dad went on the buying trips to Baltimore and New York. We kids always new that we would get some wonderful toys as a result of those trips. I don't know how long Dad worked for your Dad, but we moved to Atlanta in 1929. Prior to moving to Atlanta, Dad had a little grocery store down the hill from yours. It was next door to Mr. Nolan's printing shop. I was too young at the time to know the details, but I'm sure that he went bankrupt. That is when we were dirt poor but I didn't know it at the time. 

During the time that Dad worked at Hollbergs Mr. Frank would put on huge sales. He would have circulars printed and it fell Dads lot to travel all over Coweta and Fayette counties delivering those circulars, tacking them up on trees and at all cross roads., It was a real treat for me to get to go with him. I knew that come dinner time we would find a creek to cross and that is where we would pull off the road to eat our cheese and crackers and maybe a can of sardines. It seems like only yesterday. 

Mr. Frank, the newspaper article said the store was about to celebrate 100 years of operation. I think that is just fantastic. In the way of celebration, let me remind you of another celebration you had at another time. I don't recall what the occasion was, it could have been in connection with one of those sales that I mentioned above or perhaps it was in connection with your twenty-fifth anniversary. In any event the big drawing card was a money give away. At High noon on a given day during the Sale, money was going to be thrown from the second story window into the crowd gathered below. Our Dads and Mr. Weldon and I don't know who else were the throwers. Things went along fine until someone sailed a silver dollar into the crowd and hit someone in the head. No great harm was done. They said my pockets were too deep so I was not allowed near the buckets of money. By the way The money give away was no little thing. As I recall through a child's mind, there were several water buckets full of coins on all denominations. 

During the period that I've been talking about, robbery was just never heard of, however, I remember very clearly when someone broke in the back door of your store. I don't know the end result but I do remember the excitement that it caused 

Mr. Frank, our mothers are gone now, as for mine, Senoia, was her little corner of Heaven, she loved it and every one in it. I'm sure that She, Mrs. Hollberg, Kathleen McKnight, Faye Brandenburg, Mrs. Oscar Mann, Mrs. Bob Walt Freeman and too many others to mention are together tonight having a ball. I'm sure they will be watching your 100 celebration. I too will be watching for the announcement and if at all possible i am going to come. 

It is hard to realize that nearly all of my acquaintances in Senoia are gone now by death or otherwise. My reminiscing covers the period prior to 1929 which is the year the Mitchams moved to Atlanta. I think Joe Atkinson is still around. I guess Doug Hollberg is still in Griffin, I still think of Robert Sullivan as being a part of Senoia. (He Is now in terrible physical shape) Not too many of my contemporaries are left. 

Mr. Frank all I really wanted to do is say "Hi", and hope that Hollbergs has another 100 years of prosperity. 

With Sincere Best Wishes, 
[Signature] Wallace 
Wallace J. Mitcham 
171 Suburban, Dr. 
Kennesaw, Ga. 
30144

title: C. F. Hollberg Lot No. 9 Info Request Letter
description: This letter from C. F. Hollberg to one Mr Turner includes a request for information about a lot No. 9. Mr Turner replied with the requested information.
transcription:
[Body]

June. 18th. 1907

Mr Turner
Newnan. Ga.
Dear Sir.
Would you please favor me, by letting me know if lot No. 9 on Main St. Senoia Ga. a deed for this lot has ever been recorded by Mrs F. L. Watts.
Yours Truley.
C. F. Hollberg.

Mr. Hollberg,--
I have examined the record of this office and fail to locate any deed of record conveying to Mrs. Watts the lot mentioned.
Yours truely, 
[Signature "L. Turner"]

[Second Page]
[Letterhead]
OFFICE OF
W. S. HUBBARD
TAX COLLECTOR
COWETA COUNTY

[Body]
[Typed] Newnan, GA. [handwritten] April 19th 1907

[Handwritten cont.]

Mr C. F. Hollberg
Senoia, ga.

My Dear Sir & friend:
I find from examination of the digest back to 1897 [illegible]

1897... 1750
1898... 1700
1899... 1700
1900... 1300
1901... 1320
1903... 810
1904... 820
1905... 325
1906... 50

I don't find anywhere that #9 Main St. [illegible] on any of [illegible, cont.]

title: Rexall Drug Company C. R. Foster Letter to Frank Hollberg, Jr.
description: A letter containing a black and white photograph of Mr. Frank Hollberg, Jr., from C. R. Foster, the Southeastern Regional Sales Manager of Rexall Drug Company, that supplied the Hollberg pharmacy.
archive_reference: LTR_Jan. 06, 1956_Rexall Letter to Frank Hollberg Jr
transcription:
[Letterhead]
REXALL DRUG COMPANY
43 LEON STREET BOSTON, MASSACHUSETTS

ATLANTA REGION
C. R. FOSTER
SOUTHEASTERN REGIONAL SALES MANAGER
1120 OAKLEIGH DRIVE
EAST POINT, GA

[Body]
January 6, 1956
Mr. Frank Hollberg, Jr.
Hollberg's Pharmacy
Senoia, Georgia

Dear Frank:

Attached is a photograph which was taken on your recent visit to the East Point Branch. We have been trying to build up a photograph file on all of our valued Rexallites who visit us here in atlanta.

Frank, I am sorry that I was not in when you were here but want you to know that we certainly appreciate your visit and hope you will return in the near future.

I would like to take this opportunity to wish you and yours an abundance of happiness and prosperity for the New Year and with kind personal regards I am --

Sincecerly,
[Signature, "Ray"]
C. R. Foster

bas
Enclosure

[Letterhead]
YOU CAN DEPEND ON REXALL DRUG PRODUCTS
REXALL
BOSTON; CHICAGO; EAST POINT; FORT WORTH; LOS ANGELES; PITTSBURGH; PORTLAND, ORE.; SAN FRANCISCO; ST. LOUIS; LEICESTERSHIRE, ENGLAND; PORT ELIZABETH, S. AFRICA; TORONTO, CANADA

title: Hollberg House for Sale Unknown Magazine
description: A magazine article describing the sale of the Hollberg House/Hotel.
archive_reference: MIS_Hollberg House for Sale Unknown Magazine
transcription:
[Title head]
House Hunting
Unusual, historic or just pleasant houses for sale in Georgia

The Elegance of 1895
[body]
HAVE YOU EVER dreamed of running a first-class restaurant in an elegant mansion? The availability of the old Hollberg Hotel, 35 minutes south of Atlanta in the small town of Senoia, Georgia, may bring the realization of that dream.

The size of the building--17 to 19 rooms, depending on how you count alcoves, nooks, and crannies--makes it feasible for all but the most gigantic families. Yet as owner Don Hartsfield points out, the area in which Senoia is located lacks a really sophisticated dining place. Once you size up the splendidly proportioned rooms and the entrance hall with its fine curved staircase, you'll immediately see the possibilities for a comfortable eating area or many other commercial uses.

A wide porch of runs across the front and most of the way around the left side of the house. Fifteen tall white clumns suport it. All the windows contain old glass, some of those on [end]

(continued on page 55)

[photo caption]
The Hollberg House in Senoia was a private residence, then a hotel, and finally a private residence again. Imagination and capital could make it a thriving commercial enterprise once more.

title: Hollberg Department Store Ad Card
description: An ad card for the Hollberg Department Store.
transcription:
"AS A MAN THINKETH"

A MAn is literally what he thinks. his character being the complete sum of his thoughts. Man is made or unmade by himself.

Man is the master of thought, the moulder of character, and the maker and shaper of condition, environment, and destiny.

Men are anxious to improve their circumstances but unwilling to improve themselves. The man who does not shrink from self-crucifixion can never fail to accomplish the object upon which his heart is set.

There is no physician like cheerful thought for dissipating the ills of the body; there is no comforter to compare with good will for dispersing the shadows of grief.

Thoughts of doubt and fear never accomplish, and never can. They always lead to failure. Purpose, energy, power to do, and all strong thoughts cease when doubt and fear creep in. The will to do springs from the knowledge that we can do.

Hollberg's Pharmacy
Hollberg's Department Store
Senoia, Ga.

title: The Frank Hollberg Jr. Home
description: A slip of paper with handwritten corrections detailing the construction and history of the Hollberg home.
transcription:
THE FRANK HOLLBERG JR HOME

This was part of the property owned by the Barnes Family who were pioneers in the development of Senoia.

In 1873 this early American Colonial type house was built by John Addy and John Mays. The foundation of solid rock on which large 12 x 16 inch were placed for support and large cross beams size 12 x 10 inches were placed cross wise. These support beams were all hand cut and hand hewn from solid oak tim[b]er and were to be as strong to-day as they were 150 years ago.

In 1930 the Homer Hutchinsons completely re-modeled the home adding two additional rooms to the second story and re-modeling the stairway and stairwell. 

In 1942 the Frank Hollberg's purchased the house making extensive further improvements including well equip(t)ed and remodeled kitchen, breakfast room, [crossed out] Rumpus room [end of crossed out], [pencilled in] Den [end of pencil], additional bathrooms, basement and central air conditioning and heating.

title: Newspaper Article "A VISIT FROM SENATOR GAMBRELL"
description: This newspaper article from the Newnan Times-Herald describes a meeting between U.S. Senator David Gambrell and Ewing Barnett, Frank Hollberg III, and Will Haugen.
transcription: A VISIT FROM SENATOR GAMBRELL --- U.S. Senator David Gambrell, a frequent visitor to Newnan in Former years, paid his first official visit here as a Senator last Thursday morning, when he was the honored guest of the Newnan - Coweta Chamber of Commerce at a membership breakfast at Holiday Inn. Greeting the Senator, second from right, are Ewing Barnett, membership chairman; Frank Hollberg, III, president; and Will Haugen, who introduced him to the gathering.

title: Newspaper Article "FHA WEEK IN SENOIA"
description: This brief newspaper clipping describes Senoia Mayor C. Frank Hollberg, Jr. signing a proclamation declaring this week "FHA Week" in Senoia.
archive_reference: NWS_FHA Week in Senoia, Hollberg Jr
transcription: FHA WEEK IN SENOIA --- C. Frank Hollberg, Jr., mayor of Senoia, signs a proclamation declaring this week "FHA Week" in Senoia. East Coweta High School and other schools throughout the nation are observing National Future Homemakers of America Week by taking part in different projects each day. Present for the signing were Connie Arrowood, left, chairman of FHA Week, and Gale Hancock, fun day chairman.

title: Newspaper Article "Frank Hollberg Jr. Celebrates 90th Birthday"
description: This newspaper article, written by Angela Webster, describes Frank Hollberg Jr.'s life and his 90th birthday celebrations.

description: This newspaper article, written by Suzanne Dolezal, describes the Hollberg's hotel's role in the creation of Gone with the Wind.

title: Large Crowd Gathered in front of Hollberg's Drug Store
description: A black and white photo depicting a large crowd of people gathered in front of Hollberg's Drug Store. Found in miscellaneous Hollberg album.

title: Man and Woman in Garden
description: A man and a woman posed in a garden holding hands. The woman looks at the man, while the man's attention is turned to the camera. Found in miscellaneous Hollberg album.

title: Three Men Posed In Front of Newnan Water & Light Commission Truck
description: This black and white photo depicts three men posed in front of a Newnan Water & Light Commission Truck. Two of the men are standing, while one is notably in a wheelchair. Potentially members of the Hollberg family. Found in miscellaneous Hollberg album.

description: Black and white photograph of house with two chimneys. Unknown photographer. Potentially The Addy House (22 Bridge Street). Found in miscellaneous Hollberg album.

title: Marion Hollberg Memorial Photocard
description: A memorial card stating the day of death for one Marion Hollberg, three years old. It includes an illustration of angels, a poem, and a photograph. Found in miscellaneous Hollberg album.
transcription:
[Illustration of an angel carrying away a child]

In Loving Remembrance of

Marion Hollberg,
Died Aug. 22, 1900.
Age 3 years.

We had a little treasure once,
He was our joy and pride,
We loved him, ah! perhaps too well,
For soon he slept and died.
All is dark within our dwelling,
Lonely are our hearts today,
For the one we loved so dearly,
Has forever passed away.

title: My Prayer For Today C. F. Hollberg Jr.
description: A typed prayer for today, signed and dated by C. Frank Hollberg Jr. in handwriting.
transcription:
My Prayer For Today

"Keep my tongue free from the/recital of endless details. Seal my,/ lips on my aches and pains; They/increase daily and the need to/speak on them becomes almost a/compulsion. I ask for grace enough/to listen to the retelling of others/afflictions, and to be helped to/endure them with patience.

"I would like to have improved/memory, but I'll settle for growing/humility and an ability to/capitulate when my memory/clashes with the memory of others. Teach me the glorious lesson that/on some occasions I may be/mistaken.

"Keep me reasonably kind; I've/never aspired to be a saint... saints must be rather difficult to/live with... yet on the other hand,/an embittered old person is a/constant burden.

"Please give me the ability to see/good in unlikely places and talents/in unexpected people. And give me/the grace to tell them so, dear/Lord."

[handwritten]
July 10,1991
C. Frank Hollberg Jr.

title: Georgia State Senate Commendation for Mr. C. Frank Hollberg Jr.
description: A commendation from the Georgia State Senate for Mr. C. Frank Hollberg Jr. for his dedicated service to the citizens of Senoia, Georgia. It is marked with a gold seal and signed by the President of the Senate and the secretary of the Senate.
archive_reference: MIS_1981_Ga State Senate Resolution Commendation for CF Jr
transcription:
[letterhead]
Georgia State Senate

A Resolution

[body]
Commending Mr. C. Frank Hollberg, Jr.; and for other purposes.

WHEREAS, Mr. C. Frank Hollberg, Jr., has long been noted for his dedicated service to the citizens of Senoia, Georgia; and

WHEREAS, during his service as the chairman of the Coweta County Planning Commission from 1959 to 1965 and as the mayor of Senoia, Georgia, from 1974 to 1977, he devoted his time and energy to the goal of progressive and responsible government; and

WHEREAS, Mr Hollberg repeatedly demonstrated the marked level of sensitivity and responsibility ideally expected of elected officials in an effective, democratic government; and

WHEREAS, Mr. Hollberg has displayed an active concern for the welfare of the community, evidenced by his service as president of the Senoia Cemetery Association, as a member of the Senoia Historical Society, as a member of the Senoia Lions Club, as a Boy Scout Adult Leader, as a Master Mason of 32 degree, as a Shriner, and as a member of he Senoia Baptist Church.

NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED BY THE SENATE that the members of this body do hereby commend Mr. C. Frank Hollberg, Jr., for his disinguished service in governmental affairs and for his distinguished service in governmental affairs and for his active participation in numerous civic and community organizations and do hereby extend to him their best wishes in all his future pursuits.

BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED that the Secretary of the Senate is hereby authorized and directed to transmit an appropriate copy of this resolution to Mr. C. Frank Hollberg, Jr.

SENATE RESOLUTION 138
By: Senator Cobb of the 28th

Adopted in the Senate March 4, 1981

[Signed Zell Miller]
PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE

[Signed Hamilton McWhorter Jr.]
SECRETARY OF THE SENATE

title: Letter of Sale Margaret McKnight Hollberg's Department Store
description: Letter of sale from Margaret McKnight to the Hollbergs describing the sale of building 306-308 East Side Main Street, Senoia, GA. This letter is predominantly typed with a small illegible note and a signature from Margaret McKnight.
transcription:
[letterhead]
Does Not 50 Years Continuous Successful Buisness Indicate Reliability?

HOLLBERG'S
DEPARTMENT STORE
SENOIA, GEORGIA

HOLLBERG'S PHARMACY
Senoia, Georgia
HOLLBERG'S DEPARTMENT STORE
Senoia, Georgia
HOLLBERG'S HARDWARE COMPANY
Griffin, Georgia
DOUGLAS HARDWARE COMPANY
Griffin, Georgia

[body]
For and in consideration of Forty Five Hundred Dollars ($4,500.00) $100.00 of which is in hand paid, receipt of which is hereby admitted, I bargain, sell and convey to Hollberg's Senoia, Georgia, the building located at 306-308 East Side Main Street, said building now occupied by the Hollberg's Department Store. The balance of $4,400.00 on the purchase price of said building to be paid upon delivery of satisfactory deeds to said building. I will assume and pay pro-rata taxes and insurance on this building to date and Hollbergs8 will assume their pro-rata share of taxes [illegible handwriting, one word possibly "insurance"] from this date on.

Signed.
Margaret McKnight

Senoia, Georgia.
This June 14th 1945.

[letter head]
A GOOD STORE SINCE 1894

title: Main Street
description: a black and white photo of Main Street in Senoia, Ga that shows cars, telephone wires, and local businesses. The photo has some scratches and silvering. The original has a white border and measures 3" x 4."
